title: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: EBV Transmission and Epidemiology
items.content: EBV is transmitted primarily through oral secretions, with a high prevalence in adulthood (>90% seropositive). Infection timing varies by socioeconomic factors, affecting IM prevalence.
keyPoints: EBV spreads via contact with oral secretions, often from asymptomatic adults to infants or young adults.
>90% of individuals are infected and have antibodies by adulthood.
Infections peak in early childhood and late adolescence; higher hygiene standards delay infection until adulthood.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis of EBV in Infectious Mononucleosis (IM)
items.content: EBV infects B cells and epithelial cells, leading to IM characterized by lymphoid tissue enlargement and altered T-cell ratios.
keyPoints: During acute IM, up to 1% of peripheral B cells are infected; post-recovery, 1–50 per million remain infected.
Acute IM shows an inverted CD4+/CD8+ T-cell ratio with clonal expansions of EBV-specific CD8+ T cells (up to 40%).
Memory B cells serve as the reservoir for latent EBV infection.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Role of Cellular Immunity in Controlling EBV
items.content: Cellular immunity, particularly T-cell responses, is crucial for controlling EBV infection and preventing lymphoproliferative disorders.
keyPoints: Suppressor T cells, NK cells, and nonspecific cytotoxic T cells initially control EBV-infected B cell proliferation.
Human leukocyte antigen-restricted cytotoxic T cells later target EBNAs and LMPs to destroy infected cells.
Compromised T-cell immunity can lead to uncontrolled proliferation of EBV-infected B cells.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Presentation of Epstein-Barr Virus Infections Across Age Groups
items.content: EBV infections present differently depending on the age group. Infants and young children often have asymptomatic or mild pharyngitis, while ~75% of adolescents develop infectious mononucleosis (IM). Elderly patients typically experience nonspecific symptoms like prolonged fever and fatigue, with less frequent occurrence of pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and atypical lymphocytes.
keyPoints: Infants/young children: asymptomatic or mild pharyngitis
Adolescents: ~75% present as IM
Elderly: nonspecific symptoms (prolonged fever, fatigue), less frequent pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Timeline and Symptoms of Infectious Mononucleosis in Young Adults
items.content: In young adults, the incubation period for IM is ~4–6 weeks. A prodrome with fatigue, malaise, and myalgia lasts 1–2 weeks before fever, sore throat, and lymphadenopathy onset. Fever is usually low-grade, most common in the first 2 weeks but may persist >1 month. Lymphadenopathy and pharyngitis are prominent early on, while splenomegaly appears later.
keyPoints: Incubation period: ~4–6 weeks
Prodrome duration: 1–2 weeks
Fever: low-grade, most common in first 2 weeks, may persist >1 month
Lymphadenopathy and pharyngitis: prominent in first 2 weeks
Splenomegaly: more prominent during second/third weeks
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Laboratory Findings and Complications of Infectious Mononucleosis
items.content: In IM, the white blood cell count peaks at 10,000–20,000/μL during weeks two or three. Lymphocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes is common, predominantly CD8+ T cells. Low-grade neutropenia and thrombocytopenia are frequent in the first month. Liver function abnormalities occur in >90%, with mild elevations of aminotransferases and alkaline phosphatase, and ~40% have elevated bilirubin. Complications include CNS issues (e.g., meningitis), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (~2%), splenic rupture (<0.5%), and upper-airway obstruction.
keyPoints: WBC count: peaks at 10,000–20,000/μL
Lymphocytosis: >10% atypical lymphocytes (CD8+ T cells)
Liver function abnormalities: >90%
Complications: CNS issues, autoimmune hemolytic anemia (~2%), splenic rupture (<0.5%)
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: EBV-Associated Lymphoproliferative Disease and Immunodeficiency
items.content: EBV-associated lymphoproliferative disease occurs in patients with congenital or acquired immunodeficiencies, such as severe combined immunodeficiency, AIDS, and those undergoing immunosuppressive therapy post-transplant. These patients present with fever, lymphadenopathy, and gastrointestinal symptoms due to B-cell hyperplasia or lymphoma.
keyPoints: Occurs in immunocompromised individuals (e.g., AIDS, transplant recipients on cyclosporine).
Clinical presentation includes fever, lymphadenopathy, gastrointestinal symptoms.
Pathologic findings: B-cell hyperplasia or poly- or monoclonal lymphoma.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: X-linked Lymphoproliferative Disease and EBV
items.content: X-linked lymphoproliferative disease is a recessive disorder in young boys, leading to fatal lymphoproliferative disorders post-EBV infection. Most patients succumb to acute IM or develop conditions like hypogammaglobulinemia, malignant B-cell lymphomas, aplastic anemia, or agranulocytosis.
keyPoints: Recessive disorder affecting young boys with normal childhood infection response.
Fatal outcomes post-EBV infection include acute infectious mononucleosis (IM).
Associated conditions: hypogammaglobulinemia, malignant B-cell lymphomas, aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: EBV and Malignancies
items.content: EBV is linked to several malignancies, including Burkitt's lymphoma, anaplastic nasopharyngeal carcinoma, gastric carcinoma, Hodgkin's lymphoma, and non-Hodgkin's lymphomas in AIDS patients. EBV DNA presence and antibody titers are diagnostic markers.
keyPoints: Burkitt's lymphoma: ~15% in the US, ~90% in Africa associated with EBV.
Anaplastic nasopharyngeal carcinoma: Common in southern China, uniformly associated with EBV.
Gastric carcinoma: 9% are EBV-positive; >90% of gastric lymphoepithelioma-like carcinomas are EBV-associated.
Hodkin's lymphoma: Elevated antibody titers to EBV; viral DNA found in Reed-Sternberg cells in ~50% of US cases.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: EBV's Association with CNS Lymphomas and Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) is significantly associated with certain malignancies and neurological conditions. Virtually all central nervous system (CNS) lymphomas in AIDS patients are linked to EBV, indicating a strong correlation between the virus and this type of cancer in immunocompromised individuals.
keyPoints: Virtually all CNS lymphomas in AIDS patients are associated with EBV.
EBV is linked to an increased risk of multiple sclerosis (MS) after primary infection; epidemiological studies show a 32-fold higher risk post-primary EBV infection compared to other viruses.
A history of infectious mononucleosis and elevated antibodies to Epstein-Barr nuclear antigen (EBNA) are more common in individuals with MS.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.title: EBV's Role in Tumorigenesis
items.content: EBV is implicated in the pathogenesis of various tumors, particularly in immunocompromised patients. The virus has been detected in B cells from lymphomatoid granulomatosis lesions and in smooth-muscle tumors among organ transplant recipients.
keyPoints: EBV DNA found in tumors such as angiocentric nasal NK/T-cell lymphoma, aggressive NK leukemia/lymphoma, T-cell lymphoma, CNS lymphoma, leiomyosarcomas (in AIDS patients), and smooth-muscle tumors (in organ transplant recipients).
Further research is needed to fully understand EBV's role in these conditions.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.title: Heterophile Antibody Testing for Infectious Mononucleosis (IM)
items.content: The heterophile antibody test is a primary diagnostic tool for IM, with a titer of ≥40 being diagnostic in symptomatic patients. The monospot test offers higher sensitivity and specificity than the classic heterophile test.
keyPoints: Heterophile antibody titer ≥40 is diagnostic for acute EBV infection if symptoms are compatible with IM and atypical lymphocytes are present.
Monospot test has ~75% sensitivity and ~90% specificity compared to EBV-specific serologies.
False positives in monospot tests can occur in patients with connective tissue disease, lymphoma, viral hepatitis, and malaria.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: EBV-Specific Antibody Testing for Acute Infection
items.content: For diagnosing acute EBV infection when heterophile antibodies are absent, testing for IgM and IgG to VCA is crucial. Seroconversion to EBNA positivity confirms acute infection.
keyPoints: IgM antibody to VCA is elevated in >90% of patients at disease onset and is most useful during the first 2–3 months.
IgG antibody to VCA indicates past exposure as it persists for life.
EBNA antibodies become detectable 3–6 weeks after symptom onset and persist lifelong, except in immunodeficient patients or those with chronic active EBV.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Infectious Mononucleosis
items.content: While ~90% of IM cases are due to EBV, 5–10% result from CMV. Differential diagnosis is essential for heterophile-negative mononucleosis.
keyPoints: CMV is the most common cause of heterophile-negative mononucleosis.
Differential diagnosis should consider other causes when heterophile tests are negative.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which of the following is a common transmission route for Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)?
items.explanation: EBV is spread by contact with oral secretions, frequently transmitted through saliva during kissing.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: In which group of individuals is oropharyngeal shedding of EBV most increased?
items.explanation: Shedding of EBV is increased in immunocompromised patients and those with infectious mononucleosis.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What role does cellular immunity play in controlling EBV infection?
options: It is less important than humoral immunity.
Suppressor T cells and NK cells are crucial in the initial phase.
Only natural killer (NK) cells control the proliferation of infected B cells.
Humoral immunity alone controls the infection.
items.explanation: Cellular immunity, including suppressor T cells, NK cells, and nonspecific cytotoxic T cells, is more important than humoral immunity in controlling EBV infection.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which cell type serves as the reservoir for Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) in the body?
items.explanation: Memory B cells, not epithelial cells, are the reservoir for EBV in the body.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most common in adolescents with Epstein-Barr Virus infection?
items.explanation: Approximately 75% of EBV infections in adolescents present as Infectious Mononucleosis.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical duration of fever in most patients with Infectious Mononucleosis?
items.explanation: Fever usually lasts for 1–2 weeks and may persist for more than 1 month in some cases.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which lymph node group is most often affected by lymphadenopathy during Infectious Mononucleosis?
items.explanation: Lymphadenopathy in IM most often affects the posterior cervical nodes.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of these is a common laboratory finding during the second or third week of Infectious Mononucleosis?
options: Elevated lymphocyte count with >10% atypical lymphocytes
items.explanation: The white blood cell count is usually elevated, and there is demonstrable lymphocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which protein is associated with most cases of X-linked lymphoproliferative disease?
items.explanation: SAP, encoded by SH2D1A, is the protein associated with most cases of X-linked lymphoproliferative disease.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which malignancy has a high association rate with EBV in Africa?
items.explanation: ~90% of Burkitt's lymphoma cases in Africa are associated with EBV.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which marker is useful for screening nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
items.explanation: Antibody to the EBV protein BNLF1 in serum is a useful screening marker for nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of gastric lymphoepithelioma-like carcinomas are EBV-positive?
items.explanation: >90% of gastric lymphoepithelioma-like carcinomas are EBV-positive.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which type of lymphoma is virtually always associated with EBV in AIDS patients?
items.explanation: Virtually all CNS lymphomas in AIDS patients are associated with EBV.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: In which condition has an epidemiology study found a 32-fold higher risk after primary EBV infection?
items.explanation: An epidemiology study found that the risk of multiple sclerosis was 32-fold higher after primary EBV infection.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: In which patient population is viral DNA detected in smooth-muscle tumors?
items.explanation: Studies have demonstrated viral DNA in smooth-muscle tumors from organ transplant recipients.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.question: Which test is more sensitive for detecting heterophile antibodies compared to the classic heterophile test?
items.explanation: The monospot test is more sensitive than the classic heterophile test.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the diagnostic threshold for a heterophile antibody titer in acute EBV infection?
items.explanation: A titer of ≥40 is diagnostic of acute EBV infection.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: When are IgM antibodies to viral capsid antigen (VCA) most useful in diagnosing acute infectious mononucleosis?
options: During the first 2–3 months of disease
Only during the third week of illness
items.explanation: IgM antibody to VCA is most useful for diagnosis of acute IM because it is present at elevated titers only during the first 2–3 months.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which antibodies are not detectable in children under 5 years old, the elderly, or those with atypical symptoms of infectious mononucleosis?
items.explanation: Heterophile antibodies are usually not detectable in children <5 years of age, the elderly, or patients with atypical symptoms.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: EBV is transmitted by airborne droplets.
items.explanation: EBV is spread by contact with oral secretions and not through airborne droplets.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: In areas with higher standards of hygiene, EBV infection is often delayed until adulthood.
items.explanation: In regions with higher hygiene standards, EBV infection tends to be delayed until adulthood.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Penicillin-associated rashes in EBV infections are predictive of future adverse reactions to penicillins.
items.explanation: Penicillin-associated rashes are not predictive of future adverse reactions to penicillins.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Splenomegaly is more prominent during the first two weeks of Infectious Mononucleosis.
items.explanation: Splenomegaly is more prominent during the second and third weeks of the illness.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Chronic fatigue syndrome is caused by persistent EBV infection.
items.explanation: Patients with chronic fatigue syndrome do not have elevated levels of EBV DNA in the blood, indicating that it is not caused by persistent EBV infection.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: EBV reactivation has been definitively established as a cause of post-acute COVID-19 syndrome.
items.explanation: It is unclear that EBV is a cause of the symptoms of post-acute COVID-19 syndrome.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: EBV is associated with an increased risk of multiple sclerosis after primary infection.
items.explanation: An epidemiology study found a 32-fold higher risk of multiple sclerosis after primary EBV infection.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: EBV DNA has been detected in tumors from immunocompetent patients with B-cell lymphoma.
items.explanation: EBV DNA is associated with angiocentric nasal NK/T-cell lymphoma, aggressive NK leukemia/lymphoma, T-cell lymphoma, and CNS lymphoma, not specifically B-cell lymphoma.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 3)

items.statement: The presence of IgG antibodies to VCA is useful for diagnosing acute infectious mononucleosis.
items.explanation: IgG antibody to VCA usually is not useful for diagnosis of IM but indicates past exposure.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: EBV DNA detection in blood can be useful if serologic studies yield equivocal results.
items.explanation: Detection of high levels of EBV DNA in blood may help when serologic tests are inconclusive.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: Immunocompromised patients have increased shedding of EBV.
items.reason: The virus persists in B cells even when treated with acyclovir.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the increased shedding.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: The CD21 receptor on B cells is involved in EBV entry.
items.reason: CD21 serves as a co-receptor for human leukocyte antigen class II during EBV infection.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason is false; CD21 is not a co-receptor for HLA class II.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · Epstein-Barr Virus Infections, Including Infectious Mononucleosis | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: The severity of Infectious Mononucleosis correlates with levels of CD8+ T cells and EBV DNA in the blood.
items.reason: CD8+ T cells predominate among atypical lymphocytes during the illness.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the predominance of CD8+ T cells explains the correlation with disease severity.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Deaths from Infectious Mononucleosis are most often due to bacterial superinfection.
items.reason: Complications such as CNS issues, splenic rupture, and upper-airway obstruction can also cause deaths.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; deaths are very rare and usually due to CNS complications, splenic rupture, or upper-airway obstruction.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Malaria may enhance the proliferation of B cells with elevated EBV DNA in Burkitt's lymphoma.
items.reason: Malaria impairs cellular immunity to EBV and induces polyclonal B-cell activation, increasing germinal center activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Chronic active EBV infection is common.
items.reason: It involves elevated levels of EBV DNA in blood for more than three months with organ involvement.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; chronic active EBV infection is very rare.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Antibodies to early antigen diffuse (EA-D) persist for only 3–6 months.
items.reason: These antibodies usually persist for a short duration and are elevated in severe disease cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why EA-D antibodies persist briefly.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Culture of EBV from throat washings is helpful in diagnosing acute infection.
items.reason: EBV persists in the oropharynx for the lifetime of an infected individual.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because culture is not helpful, while the reason correctly states EBV's persistence.
items.reference: Epstein Barr Virus Infections Including Infectious Mononucleosis · DIAGNOSIS
